From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

**Symeon** (or **Simeon**) **of Durham** (fl. c.1090 to c. 1128[[1]](#cite_note-dnbsymeon-1)) was an [English chronicler](/wiki/English_historians_in_the_Middle_Ages "English historians in the Middle Ages") and a [monk](/wiki/Monk "Monk") of [Durham Priory](/wiki/Durham_Priory "Durham Priory"). He is known for two historical works which are particularly valuable for northern affairs, about the church of Durham and the history of England.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Symeon_of_Durham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Symeon was a [Benedictine monk](/wiki/Benedictine_order "Benedictine order") at [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral") at the end of the eleventh century. He may have been one of 23 monks moved to Durham from the monastery at [Jarrow](/wiki/Jarrow "Jarrow") by Bishop [William of St Calais](/wiki/William_de_St-Calais "William de St-Calais") in 1083, but the historian Bernard Meehan thinks that it is more likely that Symeon entered Durham in the 1090s.[[1]](#cite_note-dnbsymeon-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) He eventually became [precentor](/wiki/Precentor "Precentor") of the [priory](/wiki/Priory "Priory"), and examples of his handwriting appear to survive in several Durham books, including the *[Liber Vitae](/wiki/Durham_Liber_Vitae "Durham Liber Vitae")*, the so-called Cantor's Book (whose text he would have had to keep up to date as part of his duties as precentor), and in copies of his own historical works.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Symeon_of_Durham&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Works")]

Symeon was author of two historical works which are particularly valuable for northern affairs, the *[Libellus de Exordio](/wiki/Libellus_de_Exordio "Libellus de Exordio") atque Procursu istius, hoc est Dunelmensis, Ecclesie* (*The Little Book on the Origins and Progress of this Church, that is of Durham*) and a historical compilation *Historia regum Anglorum et Dacorum*. The *[Libellus de Exordio](/wiki/Libellus_de_Exordio "Libellus de Exordio")*, a history of the community of Durham (originally settled at [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne")) from its inception to the year 1096, is by far his most important work. Composed between 1104 and 1107, Symeon's task (imposed on him by his monastic superiors) was to demonstrate the continuity of Durham's history despite the notable disruptions the community weathered during the [Viking invasions](/wiki/Viking_invasions_of_england "Viking invasions of england") and even more recently in the [Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_conquest_of_England "Norman conquest of England"). Symeon sought furthermore to justify William of Saint-Calais's expulsion of Durham's clerical community in 1083, in order to replace it with a group of [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") monks drawn from Wearmouth and Jarrow. Like earlier Durham writers, Symeon finds historical continuity between the major phases of the community's development in the constant presence of their patron, [Saint Cuthbert](/wiki/Cuthbert_of_Lindisfarne "Cuthbert of Lindisfarne"). The [miracles](/wiki/Miracle "Miracle") worked in Cuthbert's name during the late Anglo-Saxon period were particularly flamboyant, and the *Libellus* contains engaging accounts of some of these, including the miracle of the three waves (when Cuthbert turned a portion of the Irish Sea into blood in order to prevent his followers from taking his relics out of England, see *Libellus* ii.11), the foundation of Durham (when Cuthbert's body, being moved across England on a cart, refused to be moved, signalling his desire to remain at Durham, see *Libellus* iii.1), and several picturesque deaths visited upon the enemies of Cuthbert's devotees.

Several versions of the *Libellus* survive from the Middle Ages. Symeon's own revised copy can be found in Durham, University Library, Cosin V.II.6. It is this text which has been most often published. The Durham manuscript also contains two anonymous continuations of Symeon's work. The first carries the history from 1096 to the death of [Ranulf Flambard](/wiki/Ranulf_Flambard "Ranulf Flambard") (1129); the second extends from 1133 to 1144. A Cambridge manuscript ([Cambridge University Library](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Library "Cambridge University Library"), Ff. i.27) contains a third continuation covering the years 1145-1154. Another [manuscript](/wiki/Manuscript "Manuscript") (London, [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library"), [Cotton Faustina](/wiki/Cotton_Faustina "Cotton Faustina") A.V) seems to represent the text of the *Libellus* before the revisions found in the Durham manuscript.[[4]](#cite_note-4) A full list of manuscripts can be found on the *[Libellus de Exordio](/wiki/Libellus_de_Exordio "Libellus de Exordio")* page.

About 1129 Symeon undertook to write a *[Historia regum Anglorum et Dacorum](/wiki/Historia_regum_Anglorum_et_Dacorum "Historia regum Anglorum et Dacorum")*. This begins at the point where the *Ecclesiastical History* of [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") ends. Up to 957 Simeon merely copies some old Durham annals, not otherwise preserved, which are of value for northern history; from that point to 1119 he copies [John of Worcester](/wiki/John_of_Worcester "John of Worcester") with certain interpolations. The section dealing with the years 1119-1129 is, however, an independent and practically contemporaneous narrative. Symeon writes, for his time, with ease and perspicuity; but his chief merit is that of a diligent collector and [copyist](/wiki/Copyist "Copyist").[[5]](#cite_note-eb-5)

Symeon also wrote brief biographies of the [archbishops of York](/wiki/Archbishops_of_York "Archbishops of York") and a letter on the errors of [Origen](/wiki/Origen "Origen"). Other writings have been attributed to his pen, but on no good authority. They are printed in the *Historiae Anglicanae Scriptores Decem* of [Roger Twysden](/wiki/Roger_Twysden "Roger Twysden") (1652). The most complete modern edition is that of [Thomas Arnold](/wiki/Tom_Arnold_(literary_scholar) "Tom Arnold (literary scholar)") ([Rolls series](/wiki/Rolls_series "Rolls series"), 2 vols., 1882–1885). For the *Libellus*, now see Rollason's 2000 edition.

The value of the *[Old Northumbrian Annals](/wiki/Northern_Annals "Northern Annals")*, which Symeon used for the *Historia regum*, has been discussed by John Hodgson-Hinde in the preface to his *Symeonis Dunelmensis opera*, vol. i. pp. xiv. ff. (1868); by R. Pauli in *Forschungen zur deutschen Geschichte*, xii. pp. 137 sqq. (Göttingen, 1872); and by [William Stubbs](/wiki/William_Stubbs "William Stubbs") in the introduction to [Roger of Hoveden](/wiki/Roger_of_Hoveden "Roger of Hoveden"), vol. i. p. x. ("Rolls" series).[[5]](#cite_note-eb-5)
